dashboard.title: Dashboard
dashboard.onlineHint: Users that transferred data in the last 5 minutes. mita can't tie live sessions to users, so this is inferred from per-user traffic.
dashboard.rawConnectionsNote: Low-level proxy connections reported by mita. These are TCP/UDP sockets and are not linked to a specific user — use the Users page to see who is online.

users.title: Users
users.name: Name
users.nameHelp: The login this person will connect with. Pick anything — it's only used to tell users apart. It can't be renamed later; you'd create a new user instead.
users.passwordHelp: The password this person's app connects with. A strong random one is pre-filled — regenerate it or type your own. You won't need it anywhere else: it goes into the share link/QR automatically. Changing it cuts off configs you've already handed out.
users.allowPrivateHelp: Off (recommended): the user can only reach normal internet sites. On: they can also reach the server's local network (addresses like 192.168.x.x) — only needed for special setups, e.g. accessing a home NAS through the proxy.
users.quotasHelp: Traffic limits. '10240 MB per 30 days' means: once the user transfers 10 GB within the last 30 days, they're paused until older usage slides out of that window. No quotas = unlimited.
users.emptySub: Add a user to start the proxy and hand out a connection link.
users.statusLegend: Status is inferred from per-user traffic: online = data in the last 5 min, offline = used before, never = no traffic yet.

network.title: Network
network.managedAlert: Port bindings are managed by the PROXY_PORTS environment variable and reset on restart. Edit that (and the published ports) to change them.
network.infoAlert: Ports must be between 1025 and 65535 and open in the server firewall / published by Docker. One port is enough; a range is for port-hopping. Applying changes briefly restarts proxying.
network.portBindingsHelp: The 'doors' on the server that client apps connect to. Each port here must also be opened in the firewall and published in Docker, or nobody can get in. One port is enough. A range like 2012-2022 lets apps hop between ports — useful when a censor slows individual ports down.
network.mtuHelp: Maximum packet size for UDP traffic (1280–1500). Leave at 1400 unless connections freeze or stall — then try lowering to 1280. That happens when the server sits behind a VPN or tunnel that only passes smaller packets.
network.loggingLevelHelp: How chatty the proxy's log is. INFO is right for everyday use. DEBUG and TRACE record much more detail — turn them on only while hunting a problem, then switch back.
network.applyBody: Applying port changes briefly restarts proxying and drops active connections. Continue?

outbounds.title: Routing
outbounds.proxiesAlert: SOCKS5 hops for chaining. Reference them by name in the rules below.
outbounds.geoAlert: Add xray-format datasets, then match their categories in the rules below. GeoIP lists IP ranges (countries, blocked ranges); GeoSite lists domains (services like Telegram, YouTube). Common use: keep your country DIRECT and send everything else through a proxy, or send only blocked ranges/services through a proxy. You can also mount your own .dat files into the datasets directory (see docs). Large categories expand to many entries, which enlarges the server config.
outbounds.kindGeoipHint: IP-range lists (geoip.dat) — countries, blocked ranges, service networks.
outbounds.kindGeositeHint: Domain lists (geosite.dat) — matched as suffixes. Regex and keyword entries are skipped (mieru can't match them).
outbounds.rulesAlert: Evaluated top to bottom; the first match wins. If nothing matches, traffic goes out directly. Use * to match everything.
outbounds.proxyNameHelp: A short label of your choice, e.g. 'exit'. You'll pick this name in routing rules to say 'send traffic through it'. Renaming breaks rules that use the old name.
outbounds.hostHelp: Where the next proxy server lives: its IP address (1.2.3.4) or domain (proxy.example.com). Traffic matching a PROXY rule is forwarded there.
outbounds.portHelp: The port that proxy accepts connections on. SOCKS5 servers usually use 1080 — check what yours says.
outbounds.socksAuthHelp: Only fill these in if the proxy you're connecting to requires a login. Leave empty otherwise.

chain.title: Chain
chain.upstreamAlert: Route this panel's egress through another mieru panel. Paste a peer key generated by the other panel; this panel runs a client to it and exposes it as a proxy you can reference in Routing rules (e.g. send everything, or only certain traffic, through it).
chain.peerNameHelp: A label of your choice for the other server, e.g. 'exitNode'. It appears under this name as a proxy in Routing rules.
chain.peerKeyHelp: A long mieru:// code generated on the other panel ('Generate peer key' there). Paste it as-is. It contains that server's address and login — anyone who has it can route traffic through that server, so don't post it anywhere public.
chain.connected: Peer connected. It is now available as a proxy in Routing rules.
chain.asUpstreamTitle: Use this panel as an upstream
chain.asUpstreamText: Generate a key and paste it into another panel's Upstream panels to let it route through this server. The key contains credentials, so share it privately.
chain.keyModalText: Paste this into the other panel. User {name} was created here for the chain. Treat it as a secret.

advanced.title: Advanced
advanced.dualStackHelp: Some sites have both an old-style (IPv4) and new-style (IPv6) address. This picks which one the server uses. Leave the default unless some sites don't open — 'Only IPv4' often fixes servers with broken IPv6.
advanced.hostsHelp: Manual 'this site lives at this address' entries. When a user opens the domain on the left, the server goes straight to the IP on the right, skipping the DNS lookup. Rarely needed — mainly for internal services or overriding a wrong DNS answer.
advanced.obfuscationAlert: Fine-tunes how mieru traffic looks on the wire to resist detection. Defaults are good for most setups — change these only if you know your censor's behavior. Settings apply without dropping connections.
advanced.seedHelp: A number that fixes the 'style' of automatic disguise, so it stays the same across restarts. Two servers with the same seed disguise traffic the same way. Leave empty unless you specifically want that.
advanced.unlockAllHelp: Off: the automatic disguise sticks to a conservative, well-tested set of tricks. On: it may use every trick available, which can look more exotic — sometimes better against censors, sometimes worse. When in doubt, leave off.
advanced.tcpFragmentHelp: Chops data into smaller pieces before sending, so the traffic 'shape' is harder to recognize. Can help if connections are being detected and cut; the cost is a slightly slower connection.
advanced.maxSleepHelp: The longest random pause between two pieces, in milliseconds (0–100). Bigger pauses look more like a human browsing but slow things down. Empty = a sensible default.
advanced.noncePatternHelp: Controls what the very first bytes of each encrypted packet look like. 'Printable' modes make them resemble ordinary text protocols; 'Fixed' lets you supply your own byte prefix. Leave Random unless your censor targets this.
advanced.allUDPHelp: Apply the disguise to every UDP packet instead of only the first one of a connection. More thorough, slightly more overhead.
advanced.nonceBytesHelp: How many of those first bytes get rewritten (0–12): a random amount between min and max is used. Empty = defaults.
advanced.hexHint: Hex strings without 0x; each is a nonce prefix of up to 12 bytes. When several are listed, a random one is used per packet. E.g. 16030100 mimics a TLS handshake byte sequence.
advanced.paddingHelp: Adds random filler bytes so packets don't have telltale sizes. 'Middle' filler goes inside a packet, 'end' filler at its end. 0 turns that filler off; empty keeps mieru's default. Only worth touching if your censor profiles packet sizes.
advanced.metricsIntervalHelp: How often the proxy writes a traffic summary into its log — e.g. 30s, 5m, 2h. Purely informational; empty keeps the default.
advanced.userHintHelp: Extra protection against probing: the server ignores any packet that doesn't identify its user, instead of trying to decrypt it. Recommended on — the only downside is that very old mieru apps (before 2023) can't connect.

settings.title: Settings
settings.serverIntro: Two different addresses. Public host = where your users' apps connect (the proxy). Panel URL = where you open this admin panel. They're often different: users connect to an IP:port, while you reach the panel through a domain.
settings.publicHostHint: Where users' apps connect — goes into share links and configs.
settings.publicHostHelp: The address client apps will connect to — your server's public IP (e.g. 203.0.113.10) or a domain pointing at it. Just the address itself, no https:// prefix. It gets baked into every share link and config you hand out. Set it once here (or via the PUBLIC_HOST env var).
settings.panelUrlHint: Where you open this panel — leave empty if you use an IP.
settings.panelUrlHelp: The address you open this panel at, e.g. https://vpn.example.com — used to build share links. Setting it also locks the panel to that address, blocking requests sent to any other name. Leave empty if you access the panel by IP. Can also be set via the PANEL_URL env var.
settings.restartPendingBody: Path settings have changed but the panel is still running the old ones. Use Save & restart panel below to apply them.
settings.pathsAlert: Serve the admin panel under a secret prefix (obscurity), and place share links on a separate public prefix so they don't reveal it. Changes take effect after restarting the panel. Leave the base path empty to serve at the root.
settings.basePathHelp: A secret piece of the address the panel hides behind, e.g. /a7Fq2xK9 — the panel then only opens at https://your-host/a7Fq2xK9/, and bots scanning the root find nothing. Make it random, not a word like /admin. Empty = panel at the root. Takes effect after a panel restart.
settings.sharePathHelp: The address prefix for share links, e.g. /s — links look like https://your-host/s/abc123. It's separate from the panel path on purpose: people you share with never learn where the panel lives. Takes effect after a panel restart.
settings.subPathHelp: The address prefix for subscription links, e.g. /sub — links look like https://your-host/sub/abc123. Must differ from the panel and share paths. Takes effect after a panel restart.
settings.subPortHelp: Serve subscriptions on a dedicated port with its own listener - the admin panel and share pages stay off it. Empty = same port as the panel. Takes effect after a panel restart.
settings.subsAlert: Where the permanent subscription links (Share dialog → Subscription) are served. Changes take effect after restarting the panel; already-issued links keep working and move to the new address only after you re-share them.
settings.subPortTls: A dedicated port requires TLS, because subscriptions contain user passwords: set SUB_TLS_CERT/SUB_TLS_KEY in .env (falls back to the panel certs). If a TLS-terminating reverse proxy fronts this port, set SUB_INSECURE_HTTP=1 instead.
settings.restartBody: The panel briefly goes offline and comes back with the current settings applied. This works when the container has a restart policy (the default compose does).
settings.logoutNote: Changing credentials logs out every session, including this one.
settings.languageHelp: Interface language of this panel. Stored in this browser only.

backup.title: Backup & restore
backup.intro: Download a full, portable snapshot of the panel: users and passwords, settings, mita config, routing, GeoIP datasets and chain peers. Restore it on any server to move the whole setup. It does NOT include your .env (that lives on the host — keep it separately). Treat the file as a secret; it contains credentials.
backup.passphraseHelp: Optional. If set, the backup is encrypted (AES-256) and the same passphrase is required to restore. Recommended, since the backup contains plaintext user passwords.
backup.passphraseOptional: optional — leave empty for an unencrypted archive
backup.restoreHeading: Restore from a backup file (replaces all current data, then restarts the panel).
backup.passphraseIfEncrypted: required only if the backup is encrypted
backup.restoreConfirm: This overwrites all current panel data (users, settings, config) with the backup and restarts the panel. Continue?

share.title: Connect: {name}
share.linkText: Generate a link that expires, so you don't have to send raw credentials around. Anyone with the link can view this user's config until it expires.
share.subIntro: A permanent link the user adds once - their app refreshes the config automatically, and it keeps working after a password change. One active link per user; you can revoke or replace it at any time.
share.subHint_clash: For Mihomo Party, Clash Verge Rev, FlClash, ClashMi and other apps on the mihomo kernel (≥ 1.19.0). Add this URL as a profile/subscription - it imports ready to use.
share.subHint_mierus: For NekoBox or Exclave with the mieru plugin: add this URL as a subscription (raw link list).
share.subHint_mieru: The same subscription as a single mieru:// link (base64 list) - for apps that import mieru:// subscriptions.
share.advHint_mierus: Human-readable link, one per server. Paste or scan it in any mieru-capable app.
share.advHint_mieru: Full-fidelity config link (base64). Paste or scan it in any mieru-capable app.
share.advHint_json: Complete client config for the official mieru CLI: save and run `mieru apply config <file>`.
share.subRotateConfirm: A new link is issued and the current one stops working in every client that uses it. Continue?
share.subRevokeConfirm: The link stops working in every client that uses it. Continue?
share.subUpdates: On each refresh the client pulls the current server address, port(s), password and transport settings for this user — so rotating ports or resetting the password reaches everyone automatically, without resending links.
